issue_title: Growth, Water Scarcity, and Long-Term Supply in Nevada

inventory.set_provenance_note: This set was discovered through water-related keyword searches of NELIS/OpenStates for the 2019 (80th), 2021 (81st), 2023 (82nd) and 2025 (83rd) regular sessions. It is not a proven complete universe of water-relevant bills; 70 of 88 bills pass a strong water-title filter, and the other 18 touch water only partially or incidentally.

themes.theme: Who gets water and how permits are decided
themes.description: Rules for who may take and use Nevada's water: permit applications, deadlines, temporary changes to existing rights, and exemptions.

themes.theme: Rules for underground water and wells
themes.description: Groundwater-specific rules: over-pumped basins, groundwater boards, well drilling and replacement, and paying for well supervision.
Getting people and businesses to use less water: conservation plans, water-loss audits, efficient fixtures, and limits on decorative grass.

themes.theme: Planning for growth and future water supply
themes.description: Preparing for growth and drought: local water resource plans, tying development approvals to water, and long-range studies and advisory bodies.

themes.theme: Colorado River and deals with other states
themes.description: Nevada's stake in shared rivers and lakes: the Colorado River Commission, interstate compacts, and asks of the federal government.

themes.theme: Money for water projects and agencies
themes.description: Appropriations and grant programs for dams, water systems, river channels, studies, and the state water agency.
Keeping water safe to drink and waterways clean: pollution permits, drinking-water systems, septic-to-sewer conversion, and disclosure of well quality.

themes.theme: Who referees water disputes (courts and the State Engineer's office)
themes.description: Who checks the State Engineer's decisions and how disputes get resolved: court review, decision standards, reporting, and old claims.
Bills the keyword search caught that touch water only in passing (boats, boilers, highway names, food regulation, and similar).

bills.title: Revises provisions governing water conservation. (BDR 48-798)
bills.plain_topic: Required water utilities to report water-loss audits with their conservation plans and required WaterSense-certified fixtures in most new construction.
bills.theme: Saving water (conservation rules and incentives)

bills.title: Revises provisions related to water. (BDR 48-45)
bills.plain_topic: Let counties use general funds, not only special property assessments, to pay well-supervision costs.
bills.theme: Rules for underground water and wells

bills.title: Requires the Desert Research Institute to conduct a study concerning water treatment and recycling. (BDR S-901)
bills.plain_topic: Would have required the Desert Research Institute to study water treatment and recycling and report to the next Legislature.
bills.theme: Planning for growth and future water supply
bills.most_recent_action: (Pursuant to Joint Standing Rule No. 14.3.2, no further action allowed.)

bills.title: Revises provisions governing water. (BDR 48-214)
bills.plain_topic: Would have let the State Engineer require monitoring-and-mitigation plans, with public hearings, before deciding water permit applications.
bills.theme: Who gets water and how permits are decided
bills.most_recent_action: (Pursuant to Joint Standing Rule No. 14.3.4, no further action allowed.)

primary_sponsors.name: Assembly Committee on Natural Resources, Agriculture, and Mining

bills.title: Revises provisions governing the management of water. (BDR 48-213)
bills.plain_topic: Would have required rules for managing groundwater and surface water together (conjunctive management), including mitigation plans.

bills.most_recent_action: (Pursuant to Joint Standing Rule No. 14.3.1, no further action allowed.)

majority_yes_but_not_enacted.plain_topic: Would have required, not just encouraged, the State Engineer to use best available science, and recognized connections between groundwater and surface water.
majority_yes_but_not_enacted.note: Won at least one final-passage floor vote with majority Yes but never became law.

majority_yes_but_not_enacted.theme: Rules for underground water and wells
majority_yes_but_not_enacted.plain_topic: Would have changed how groundwater boards are established and dissolved.
majority_yes_but_not_enacted.note: Won at least one final-passage floor vote with majority Yes but never became law.

majority_yes_but_not_enacted.theme: Colorado River and deals with other states
majority_yes_but_not_enacted.plain_topic: Resolution urging the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation to consider actions and alternatives to protect and manage the Colorado River.
majority_yes_but_not_enacted.note: Resolution: passed both chambers and was enrolled; resolutions are not enacted as statutes.

majority_yes_but_not_enacted.plain_topic: Would have had a legislative interim committee review water conservation and ordered a state study of artificial turf.
majority_yes_but_not_enacted.note: Won at least one final-passage floor vote with majority Yes but never became law.

majority_yes_but_not_enacted.theme: Who referees water disputes (courts and the State Engineer's office)
majority_yes_but_not_enacted.plain_topic: Would have revised deadlines and procedures for adjudicating old 'vested' water-right claims.
majority_yes_but_not_enacted.note: Won at least one final-passage floor vote with majority Yes but never became law.

majority_yes_but_not_enacted.plain_topic: Would have banned sales of small disposable plastic water bottles in communities abutting the Lake Tahoe watershed.
majority_yes_but_not_enacted.note: Won at least one final-passage floor vote with majority Yes but never became law.

data_limits: Keyword-discovered set: the 88 bills come from water-related keyword searches, not an exhaustive sweep. Bills that affect water without water words in their titles or digests may be missing.

18 of 88 bills fail the strong water-title filter; they touch water only partially (boats, boilers, highway naming, food regulation, utility notices). Headline counts should prefer the 70-bill strong-title subset.
OpenStates detail lookups came back empty for all 88 bills, so every vote, sponsor and action record here comes from a single source: the Legislature's NELIS system.

Floor votes: 104 final-passage roll calls have full counts and named ballots. Committee records are thinner: 176 of 287 committee meeting entries (hearings and work sessions) have no recorded roll call, so most committee-stage outcomes rest on recommendations and subsequent actions, not counted votes.
Party labels on floor ballots were matched from the NELIS legislator directory with a Ballotpedia fallback (100% of 3,267 ballot rows matched); a small mismatch risk from name matching remains.
Introduced-versus-enrolled text comparisons exist only for the 48 Governor-bound bills; failed bills have no amendment-impact analysis here.

For several failed bills the processed milestone flags mark second-chamber passage even though no second-chamber final-passage roll call exists and the action history shows the bill died in the second chamber (e.g. AB387 of 2023, SB324 of 2025). Stages in this pack therefore count second-chamber passage only when a winning final-passage roll call from that chamber exists.
The pack contains no fiscal notes, agency impact studies, hearing testimony, or lobbying records; it cannot say why a bill stalled, only where.
The three 'In Progress' items are joint resolutions enrolled and delivered to the Secretary of State; they express positions or propose constitutional changes and do not become statutes.

Independent verification (data-verifier, 2026-07-16): overall PASS_WITH_WARNINGS; all bill identifiers, counts and dispositions checked; 20 of 20 sampled official URLs alive; the only warning concerned manifest file layout, not data content.
